feedback.text: The Digital Fairness Act is a vital step forward in safeguarding consumers rights in the digital sphere. But to be truly fair, it must address a glaring problem: the ability for corporations to revoke access to digital products after purchase. The Issue: Consumers routinely purchase digital games, movies, and books with the expectation of ownership. Yet these items can be rendered inaccessible without warning servers go offline, licenses expire, and content vanishes from libraries. This is not fairness. This is conditional access masquerading as ownership. Why It Matters: Imagine buying a physical book, only to have someone break into your home and remove it because the author or publisher changed their mind. Thats the digital equivalent of whats happening now. Technology makes it easier to revoke access but ease should never justify injustice. ️ The Solution: Require offline-access options for any product sold as purchased. Prohibit removing access to digital goods post-sale, regardless of publisher support status. Encourage or mandate server-independent design for video games to ensure longevity. Clarify in law that EULAs cannot override fundamental consumer rights. This is not a demand for perpetual corporate support it's a demand for permanent consumer access to what has been paid for. Ownership should not be contingent on a publishers whims or licensing contracts. Big tech has conditioned us to accept terms that would be unacceptable in any other domain.
